coredata.dc:description: The layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly technique could nanoscopically construct the composite membranes with multilayered structures. It has been frequently mentioned in the field of microelectronics, biology and clean energy, etc. In this research, low temperature proton exchange membranes (LTPEMs) were fabricated through alternate deposition of polyurethane (PU) as polyanions, Nafion and carbon dots (CDs) as polycations. The successful preparation of LTPEMs with multilayered structure has been demonstrated by the structure characterization and property analysis of the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane. The CDs and PU can combine phosphoric acid (PA) molecules owing to the hydrogen-bond interaction. The (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane was thus obtained with the combination of a large number of PA molecules. The target of enhancing the proton conductivity at subzero temperature was achieved since the well-ordered dispersion of components could reduce proton conduction resistance. Specifically, the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane exhibited the proton conductivities of (3.29 ± 3.16) × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C and (2.99 ± 0.39) × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C. Furthermore, the membranes exhibited the fine proton conductivity stability, such as 3.69 × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C and 3.34 × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C even if the membrane underwent a five-cycle heating/cooling process of −30 °C–30 °C. Furthermore, the values could reach 9.34 × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C and 1.58 × 10−2 S/cm at 0 °C after a 315 h non-stop test. The results can reveal that the LBL self-assembly technique was a prospective strategy to construct LTPEMs for the application at subzero temperature.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the layer by layer self-assembly processes (A); Photograph of the free-standing (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane (B); fluorescent photographs of CDs solution, PU and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 (C); diagrammatic structures of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 (D) and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA (E).

FTIR spectra of the CDs, Nafion, PU and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 (A), XRD curves of Nafion/CDs, (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200, (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA (B), wide scan XPS (C) and high resolution XPS spectra of various elements in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200, including F 1s (D), O 1s (E), N 1s (F), C 1s (G), S 2p (H).

Thermal stability, oxidative stability and component stability of the prepared membranes. Thermogravimetric analysis (A), scanning calorimetry curves (B), oxidative stability behaviors in Fenton reagent (C) and component leakage with humidification at 100 °C (D).

SEM and AFM images of the prepared membranes. SEM images: the CDs layer (A) and the PU layer (B) of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200; the cross-section of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 (C), (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 storing at −30 °C for 96 h; Tapping-mode AFM images of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 with the CDs layer (A) and the PU layer (B).
Weight gain, thickness swelling and volume swelling of the PA doped membranes.

Proton conductivities (A), (C) and Arrhenius plots (B), (D) of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes. (A), (B): The test during the cooling/heating processes in the range of −30 °C–30 °C; (C), (D): the test during the heating process in the range of −30 °C–160 °C.

Schematic representation of proton conduction in PU, Nafion, carbon dots and the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane. In the PU matrix, abundant water and PA molecules could form proton conduction pathways; Nafion could combine water molecules with sulfonated groups to provide proton conduction pathways; Carbon dots conduct protons with the stabilizers of carboxyl or hydroxyl groups. For the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane, PU, Nafion and CDs constituted the continuous proton conduction pathways with water and PA molecules in the range of 0°C-30 °C. However, the water molecules freeze at subzero temperature and the formed ice was piled up in membranes.
Proton conductivities of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes with the five-cycle heating/cooling process in the range of (A) −30 °C–30 °C and (B) 30 °C-60 °C.
Proton conductivities of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes at −30 °C, 0 °C and 30 °C in a 315 h non-stop test.
Mechanical properties of (A) the prepared membranes and (B) PA doped membranes, including PU/PA, Nafion/CDs/PA, (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA storing at −30 °C for 96 h.
Thickness, methanol permeability and the selectivity of the prepared membranes.

head.ce:title: Enhancing proton conductivity at subzero temperature through constructing the well-ordered structure based on carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly technique could nanoscopically construct the composite membranes with multilayered structures. It has been frequently mentioned in the field of microelectronics, biology and clean energy, etc. In this research, low temperature proton exchange membranes (LTPEMs) were fabricated through alternate deposition of polyurethane (PU) as polyanions, Nafion and carbon dots (CDs) as polycations. The successful preparation of LTPEMs with multilayered structure has been demonstrated by the structure characterization and property analysis of the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane. The CDs and PU can combine phosphoric acid (PA) molecules owing to the hydrogen-bond interaction. The (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane was thus obtained with the combination of a large number of PA molecules. The target of enhancing the proton conductivity at subzero temperature was achieved since the well-ordered dispersion of components could reduce proton conduction resistance. Specifically, the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane exhibited the proton conductivities of (3.29 ± 3.16) × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C and (2.99 ± 0.39) × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C. Furthermore, the membranes exhibited the fine proton conductivity stability, such as 3.69 × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C and 3.34 × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C even if the membrane underwent a five-cycle heating/cooling process of −30 °C–30 °C. Furthermore, the values could reach 9.34 × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C and 1.58 × 10−2 S/cm at 0 °C after a 315 h non-stop test. The results can reveal that the LBL self-assembly technique was a prospective strategy to construct LTPEMs for the application at subzero temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: For achieving the target of the carbon-neutral, various clean energy sources are experiencing the flourish development. The proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) as a highly efficient energy conversion device has been widely used in electronic devices and transportation vehicles, etc [1,2]. The proton exchange membrane (PEM) as the core component of the PEMFC determined proton conduction behaviors from the anode to the cathode and separated fuels [3]. At current, intensive attention has been paid on the development of the proton exchange membranes working in the humidification condition from the ambient temperature to 80 °C and the anhydrous condition from 80 °C to 200 °C [4–6]. Although great progress has been made on the road to commercialization of the PEMFC, the process would be tortuous by high price of the catalyst, durability, hydrogen supply system and the cold start performance, etc [7,8].

For a PEMFC, the cold start is defined as the startup from the subzero temperature to normal operation at approximately 80 °C [9]. It is recognized that the cold start is still a significant issue for the automotive industry when various fuel cell vehicles are exposed to a colder climate [10]. Therefore, many studies including the experiments and the simulation calculation have been performed for further understanding the cold start process of the fuel cell and the performance of components [11–13]. In essence, the cold start strategy is an issue of the water/heat management [14]. The produced water crowding around cathode catalyst and gas transmission channels can turn into ice while the operation was accompanied by the untimely water removal and insufficient heat generation to warm up the cell [15]. Relative to the strategy of Keep Warm, the Thaw at Start was more suitable for the vehicular application since the parking duration at subzero temperature is usually unforeseeable [15,16].

The long-term storage of the PEMFC at subzero temperature can inevitably damage the PEM, reflecting in the reduced reaction rate, membrane fragmentation and even structural failure to membrane electrode assembly (MEA) [17–19]. At present, the reports on materials with high proton-conductive property at subzero temperature are sporadic [7]. Yu HM reported the proton conductivity of 10 mS/cm at −10 °C for the aromatic-based PEM and proposed three impacts on the conductivity decrease at subzero temperature, such as free water molecules freezing, temperature dependence of proton transport through water molecules and shielding effect of ice on proton transport [20]. Jang JH has observed the enlarged cracks in the ultrathin and dense catalyst layers in 120 freeze-thaw cycles from −20 °C to 10 °C. However, the fuel cell performance was not influenced significantly [21]. Jannasch P focused on preparing the membranes based on multiblock copolymers with highly sulfonated blocks. The membranes exhibited the proton conductivity exceeding 10−2 S/cm at −20 °C [22]. Furthermore, Zhang ZJ and Xiang SC have synthesized metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with wider operation temperature from −40 °C to 100 °C [23]. The proton conductivity of the membrane reached 9.13 × 10−5 S/cm at −40 °C [24]. Zhan ZG reported the numerical analysis of the ice-induced stress in the MEA under sub-freezing operating conditions. This group believed that the mechanical damage of the MEA was related to the phase change of water from liquid to solid [25]. Furthermore, Xu G and Wang RH have prepared covalent organic gels (COGs) and porous organic polymer membranes (POPMs) with the increased proton conductivity at a lower temperature [7,26]. The acylhydrazone-based COG showed the proton conductivity of 3.8 × 10−4 S/cm at −40 °C and it was stable within 250 h [7]. Recently, the HPW-HSN@SBA-15 self-assembly with linear channels exhibited the proton conductivity of 6.8 × 10−5 S/cm at −40 °C owing to the ultrafast proton transfer without the attendance of water [27]. In our previous work, although the solution casting membranes based on PA doped sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) (SPEEK) and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) possessed enhanced proton conductivities, such as 3.86 × 10−2 S/cm at −30 °C and 8.28 × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C [28], the fine performance relied on a high content of PA molecules. Therefore, the research on constructing the LTPEMs was needed, expecting to explore the membranes with low proton conduction resistance and independence on a tremendous amount of PA molecules.

Although various proton conduction carriers have been synthesized in succession [23,24,27,29,30], the research on enhancing the proton conduction at subzero temperature has been rarely reported. In consideration of the proton conduction being confined by the disordered dispersion of components [31], we proposed the strategy of constructing the multilayered LTPEMs with a high and stable proton conductivity at subzero temperature with the LBL self-assembly technique. The (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane was prepared through the alternate deposition of three components, such as the positively charged PU, the negatively charged Nafion and CDs. The CDs are chosen because the functional stabilizers of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups can conduct protons and provide enough sites to combine PA molecules. The low price due to various sources made CDs more competitive [32]. Furthermore, it is known that the Nafion membrane is the most successful PEM both the direct application and composite membranes [33,34]. The polymer of PU was a suitable candidate as polycations because it bore many imino groups to combine PA molecules [35]. Thus, the components of PU and Nafion not only supported LBL self-assembly membranes, but also participated into the proton conduction process. Furthermore, the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane was obtained while the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane was immersed into PA solution. As expected, the enhanced proton conductivity at subzero temperature was achieved, deriving from the fast proton transfer and the multilayered microstructure hosting the membrane.

The cationic 30 wt% of PU aqueous dispersion with a molecular weight of 92,000 was received from Hepce Chem, South Korea. The 5 wt% of Nafion solution was purchased from Alfa Aesar (China) Chemical Co. Ltd. 2,6-diaminopyridine was provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Citric acid was obtained from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Concentrated sulfuric acid (98 wt%) and polyphosphoric acid (PPA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. We prepared 100 wt% PA solution by dissolving 62.6 g of PPA into 10 mL of deionized (DI) water.

The carboxylated carbon dots surrounding with carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups were prepared based on the literature [36]. Specifically, 2,6-diaminopyridine (1.5 g) and citric acid (1.5 g) were respectively dissolved into individual DI water (60 mL) and mixed well by ultrasonic oscillation at room temperature (RT) for 10 min. The homogeneous solution was stored in a teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL). Afterwards, the well-sealed autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The dark brown solution was filtered and then centrifuged for 20 min at 12 k rpm to remove the larger dots. The upper clear solution was purified via dialysis for 24 h to form the light brown anionic CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nafion and PU solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the CDs solution, the anionic 0.5 wt% Nafion solution, preparing by diluting the Nafion (5 wt%) solution was also used as polyanions. Likewise, the cationic 3 wt% of PU solution was obtained by diluting the commodity of PU (30 wt%) aqueous dispersion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of LBL self-assembly membranes and PA doped membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A normal glass slide was treated with the piranha solution (V (98 wt% sulphuric acid):V (30 wt% hydrogen peroxide) = 3:1) to form the glass substrate (GS) bearing negative charges. The GS was dipped into the component solutions of PU, Nafion, PU and CDs in succession to complete one monolayer. For the each operation, the dipping time and the rinsing time in DI water were respectively controlled in 7 min and 30 s. After repeating 200 times, the GS was completely soaked into 0.5 wt% hydrofluoric acid solution and a couple of membranes peeled off, denoting (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200. Furthermore, the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane was obtained through immersing the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane into 100 wt% PA solution in a closed conical flask for 5 min at RT. As a contrast, Furthermore, the PU/CDs and Nafion/CDs composite membranes were prepared with the solution casting method. More details about preparing the solution casting membranes were provided in supplementary information (SI).

The various characterizations on the LBL self-assembly membranes have been performed in this research. The details about the relevant equipments and the specific operation processes were provided in SI.

Fig. 1 (A) schematically represented the layer by layer self-assembly process for constructing the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane through alternate dipping a GS into PU, Nafion, PU and CDs solution. The images in Fig. S1 exhibited the transparency decrease with more layers adhering on the GS. The cycle number was determined to be 200 in consideration of the minuscule thickness increase on the deposited membranes adhering to the GS. The free-standing (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane was thus obtained (Fig. 1 (B)) with a thickness statistically 22 ± 2 μm according to the scanning electron microscope (SEM) observation. Various images of the membranes in Figure S1 (B) exhibited the fine flexibility and toughness visually. Furthermore, the fluorescent color of CDs was changed from blue to green while the CDs were deposited in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 (Fig. 1 (C)). The variation on fluorescent color was accompanied by the accumulation of CDs due to the quantum confinement effect [37]. Kotov NA has reported the red shift of luminescence because of the confinement decrease in one dimension of cadmium telluride nanoparticles [38]. Fig. 1(D) and (E) schematically presented the multilayered structures in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes. The electrostatic interaction between components guarantee the stable multilayered structure even if PA molecules were doped. Relative to the classical LBL self-assembly process with a couple of components, more components could provide the LBL self-assembly membranes with better properties, such as the fine stability, the enhanced proton conductivity and the strong mechanical strength and, etc [39].

The electrostatic interaction between components was the primary force to drive the self-assembly process. A higher absolute value of ζ is indicative of the component bearing more superficial charges, which can ensure the successful construction of LBL self-assembly membranes. Specifically, the ζ values reached −58.3 mV, −15.4 mV for 0.5 wt% of Nafion solution, 1 wt% of CDs solution; 48.9 mV for 3 wt% PU solution. Besides that, the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between components (Fig. S3), such as PU and Nafion, PU and CDs facilitated to form the stable and multilayered structure of the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane.

Fig. 2 (A) exhibited the fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) spectra of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes and the components. For PU membranes, the peaks at 1230 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 were respectively caused by the stretching vibration of C–N+ groups and the in-plane bending vibration of C–H groups. Nafion membranes exhibited the characteristic peaks at 1218 cm−1, 1143 cm−1, 1055 cm−1 and 980 cm−1 due to the –CF2-anti-symmetric stretching, C–F symmetric stretching, S–O symmetric stretching and C–F stretching vibration [40]. The –CO, –CC and C–O/C–N groups in CDs can be respectively identified by the absorption peaks at 1655 cm−1, 1387 cm−1 and 1121 cm−1. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks from the C–N+ groups (1230 cm−1), S–O groups (1018 cm−1), and –CC groups (1387 cm−1) were observed in the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane.

From Fig. 2 (B), the X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile of the CDs showed the wide (002) peak centering at 2θ = 22.8° with an interlayer spacing (d) of 0.36 nm [41]. The board peaks at 2θ = 23.4° and 17.1° were indicative of Nafion and PU membranes with amorphous structures. Moreover, the Nafion/CDs and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes respectively showed the characteristic peaks at 2θ = 17.7° and 16.3°. According to Bragg's Equation in Eq. S10, the results were indicative of the expansion of the Nafion since a large number of CDs entered into the structure and stretched the polymeric skeleton. It was speculated that CDs can penetrate the Nafion and PU layers and they were dispersed well in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes. Notably, the characteristic peak at 2θ = 16.3° shifted to a higher angle by 3.9° with PA molecules doping. It was deduced that PA molecules could occupy the space in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes. This result was in accordance with a higher weight gain of 119% but a lower volume swelling of 15.7% in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes (Fig. 5).

XPS can help to characterize the composition stability of composite membranes. From Fig. 2 (C), the wide scan XPS contained the characteristic peaks corresponding to five orbits of F1s, O1s, N1s, C1s and S2p in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 and Nafion/CDs membranes as well as three orbits of O1s, N1s and C1s in PU/CDs membranes. As shown in Fig. 2 (D) and (H), the peaks with binding energy at 291.8 eV in XPS C1s and 168.4 eV in XPS S2p were attributed to –CF2- and –SO3 - groups from Nafion. Also the membranes showed a single peak at 689.7 eV from F1s (Fig. 2 (F)). The oxygen element in –F2C-O-CF2- and –CO- groups can be confirmed by the peaks at 535.7 eV and 531.9 eV in XPS O1s (Fig. 2 (E)). The nitrogen element in -N-H of imidazole rings and –NH2 respectively corresponded to the peaks with binding energy at 399.6 eV and 402.1 eV in XPS N1s (Fig. 2 (F)). Besides that, the peaks with binding energy near 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.6 eV in XPS C1s (Fig. 2 (G)) were assigned to -C-C-, -C-N- and –CO- groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability, oxidative stability, components stability
ce:section.ce:para: The Td (5%) is defined as the temperature of the weight loss reaching to 5% [42]. Fig. 3 (A) showed that the Td (5%) values were respectively 220 °C, 240 °C and 260 °C of CDs, Nafion and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes. The quick weight loss period was observed in the range of 270–520 °C due to the decomposition of the soft segments in PU membranes [43]. For (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes, the quick weight loss period below 80 °C was assigned to the evaporation of water molecules. Besides the decomposition of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200, the additional weight loss was caused by the deformation of PA molecules [44]. According to differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curves in Fig. 3 (B), the endothermic peaks centering at 405 °C and 460 °C in PU and the peak at 425 °C in Nafion were observed in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes. Furthermore, the enthalpy change (ΔH) values were respectively 4.3 J/g, 19.53 J/g and 6.36 J/g for Nafion, PU and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes. The results were comparable with the reported ΔH values of membranes, such as 29.29 J/g of PU and 28.65 J/g of SHSPU-2 [45].

Besides thermal stability, the oxidative stability of the membrane was estimated by the weight loss and the morphology change in Fenton reagent at 68 °C. From Fig. 3 (C), the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane can keep the integrity near 25 h with a residual weight percent of 89.4%. Subsequently, some cracks and even fractures emerged and the membrane maintained a residual weight percent of 76.6% at 120 h. Although the strong oxidizers such as HO·, HOO·, Fe3+ and O2 attacked the heteroatom in molecular chains of PU and Nafion, further breached the skeleton and, ultimately, led to the fractures, the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane was not broken to pieces owing to the compact structure. Some studies have reported the oxidative stability of the PEMs, such as the residual weight percent of near 90% of cross-linked PBI membranes in Fenton reagent for 144 h at 68 °C [46]; the overall morphology of the (SPEEK/PU)210 self-assembly membrane in Fenton reagent for more than 130 h [39]; the Nafion 111 membrane with the structural defects after storing in Fenton's reagent for 48 h [47].

Fig. 3 (D) exhibited the different weight loss trends of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes with humidification at 100 °C. Specifically, there was a fast weight loss period within the initial 3 h in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes. By contrast, (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes exhibited a fluctuating weight retention without an obvious weight loss. According to the reported component stability of membranes [28,48], the initial weight loss of 10.7% was primarily caused by the PA molecules leakage. Furthermore, they respectively exhibited the weight retention values of 94.5% and 80.8% at 140 h with humidification at 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fine microstructure of the LBL self-assembly membranes
ce:section.ce:para: For (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes, the overall property was closely related with the microstructure. The SEM and atomic force microscope (AFM) images in Fig. 4 (A) and (B) and (E), (F) respectively showed the microstructure morphologies on the surface including the outer CDs, PU layers. For the CDs layer, a large number of CDs with the diameters of 200 ± 30 nm were found (Fig. 4 (A)) and the data were coincident with the AFM observation in Fig. 4 (E). The continuous dispersion of CDs is beneficial to the formation of compact structures. As regards to the PU layer, the fuzzy microphase separation structure was observed in the SEM image (Fig. 4 (B)), which was similar with the morphology of the pristine PU membrane (Fig. S5)). Furthermore, the SEM image of the cross-section view showed the multilayered structure of the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane (Fig. 4 (C)). Besides the well-ordered accumulation of PU and Nafion, the emergence of CDs among the layers demonstrated the stable existence of the CDs in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes. Furthermore, the membrane kept the multilayered structure even though it was stored at −30 °C for 96 h (Fig. 4 (D)). The AFM image in Fig. 4 (F) exhibited the clear morphology of the CDs. Although the initial CDs layer was covered by PU and Nafion layers, there was a possibility of CDs perpetrating the ultrathin layers as discussed in the XRD analysis. Furthermore, the elements of C, N, O, F and S were detected by the EDS-SEM and the fine element dispersion images were shown in Fig. S4. The results provided a support to conjecture that it is hard to shield the neighbouring Nafion and CDs layers from the upper PU.

Besides the PEMFC, the PEM with lower methanol permeability (p) and higher proton conductivity (σ) could work in the direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) [49]. In this research, a long-term test was performed and a range of p values were obtained (Fig. S6). From Table 1 , the p values of (0.710–2.11) × 10−6 cm2/s of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes were lower than (2.12–2.97) × 10−6 cm2/s of the pristine PU membrane and the reported p values of the Nafion® membranes, such as the p values of 1.8 × 10−5 cm2/s of Nafion®-117 [50], 4.9 × 10−6 cm2/s of Nafion®-115 [51] and 2.45 × 10−6 cm2/s of Nafion®-112 [52]. The decrease on the concentration (c) gradient of the methanol solutions on both sides of a membrane sample and the component leakage were the reasons to cause the continuous reduction on p values of the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes. As reported, the p values were respectively (1.11–1.31) × 10−8 cm2/s of (PVDF-CdTe-PA)5 [53], 3.3 × 10−7 cm2/s of CS/PWA [54], 0.928 × 10−6 cm2/s of Nafion/GO [55], (0.56–1.8) × 10−7 cm2/s of cross-linked SPEEK [56] and 2.9 × 10−7 cm2/s of GQD-HBM [57]. Although the multilayered structure was believed to enhance the resistance to methanol permeation, the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane exhibited higher p values since the formed hydrophilic proton conduction channels also facilitated methanol permeation. Furthermore, the selectivity (β) values, defining as the conductivity to methanol permeability [58] were (1.27–3.77) × 104 S s/cm3 for the LBL self-assembly membrane. The results are comparable with the reported β values of the mentioned membranes [53–54555657].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight gain and dimension swelling of membranes with PA doping
ce:section.ce:para: The PA doped membranes were expected to possess a suitable content of PA molecules for proton conduction as well as a lower dimension swelling for better mechanical strength. From Fig. 5 , the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane exhibited a higher weight gain of 119%, but a little higher thickness swelling of 8.36% and volume swelling of 15.7% relative to those of Nafion/CDs, PU/CDs and the pristine PU membrane. The results were indicative of the density increase in the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane. Furthermore, the weight gain and dimension swelling values of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes immersing into 40%–85% PA solutions were also measured. The increased results were consistent with higher concentrations of PA solutions and longer immersion time (Fig. S7). According to the results, the mulilayered structure accommodated more PA molecules because the continuous interfaces between layers could provide more space. The reported higher weight gain of multilayered PEMs can support the conjecture. Specifically, the weight gain values were respectively 207% of (PU/GO/PDDA/GO)200/40%PA [59], 2.23 doping level of PBI/GO/PBI/PA [60] and more than 400% of PA doped PBI with a leaf-like three-layer porous structure [61], etc.

The proton conductivity (σ) of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes in the range of −30 °C–30 °C in the heating process and cooling process was shown in Fig. 6 (A) . There was a small discrepancy on σ values, such as (2.99 ± 0.39) × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C, (1.44 ± 0.17) × 10−2 S/cm at 0 °C, (3.29 ± 3.16) × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C in the cooling process and (2.29 ± 0.30) × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C, (1.37 ± 0.11) × 10−2 S/cm at 0 °C in the heating process. The results were obviously higher than those of PU/CDs/PA membranes, such as 8.26 × 10−3 S/cm at 30 °C, 2.55 × 10−3 S/cm at 0 °C and 1.32 × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C as shown in Fig. S8. During the test process, the doped PA molecules in (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes could absorb water molecules from the environment. The relative humidity (RH) of the test cell was thus monitored and the RH values were continuously increased from (19.5 ± 0.3)% at 30 °C to (53.6 ± 0.4)% at 0 °C and stable at (50.1 ± 0.6)% at −30 °C in the cooling process. The absorbed water molecules were involved in the proton conduction even though PA molecules dominated the process in PA doped membranes. According to Arrhenius plots in Fig. 6 (B), the Ea values were respectively 23.4 kJ/mol and 24.0 kJ/mol in the heating process and the cooling process, indicating the invariable proton conductivity resistance. On the basis of a slight variation on Ea values, it was believed that the membrane maintained the stable structure without an obvious component leakage in the range of −30 °C–30 °C.

Fig. 6 (C) exhibited the discontinuity on σ values with the temperature rising from −30 °C to 160 °C. Specifically, there were four periods, including the continuous rise on σ values from −30 °C to 30 °C; then a decrease until 70 °C; a continuous rise from 70 °C to 110 °C and a gradual decrease to 160 °C. The decrease on values at 30 °C was primarily caused by the evaporation of water molecules in membranes in the consideration of the reduced RH values of the test cell from −30 °C to 30 °C. Accompanying with the increased σ values, the Ea values were respectively 25.1 kJ/mol (−30 °C–30 °C) and 24.8 kJ/mol (70 °C-110 °C). The invariable Ea values were indicative of the imperceptible change on microstructure and proton conduction mechanism. For PA doped membranes, the formed H2PO4−/HPO4 2− chains could assist the quick proton motion [62]. In our view, the reduced amount of proton conduction carriers caused the σ decline, including the water molecules evaporation in the former period and the leakage of PA molecules in the latter period.

In (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes, PA molecules dominated the proton conduction behavior from −30 °C to 160 °C. But undeniably, the absorbed water molecules participated in the proton conduction process. Fig. 7 (A) schematically described the continuous proton conduction channels consisting of amino groups in PU, sulfonated groups in Nafion and carboxyl or hydroxyl groups in carbon dots. For (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes, protons could transfer through the fast movement of PA and water molecules as proton conduction carriers from 30 °C to 0 °C (Fig. 7 (B)). However, PA molecules as the sole proton conduction carriers were in charge of the proton conduction process in the range of −30 °C–0 °C due to the state conversion of water molecules into ice (Fig. 7 (C)). The formed ice was piled up in membranes and the effect on proton conduction mechanism would be studied in the next work.

Fig. 8 (A) and (B) respectively exhibited the σ values of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes with the five-cycle heating/cooling processes of −30 °C–30 °C and 30 °C-60 °C. For the σ values at −30 °C, the initial σ value was 3.28 × 10−3 S/cm and it reached 3.69 × 10−3 S/cm after the five-cycle test. The similar situation has also been found in the σ values at 30 °C, corresponding to 2.55 × 10−2 S/cm and 3.34 × 10−2 S/cm. Notably, the maximum σ value reached 4.12 × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C in the test process, which was primarily caused by the water molecules liquefaction from the moisture. However, the σ values decreased from 1.45 × 10−2 S/cm to 8.19 × 10−3 S/cm at 60 °C (Fig. 8 (B)). In our opinion, the dynamic equilibrium on the liquefaction and evaporation of water molecules could exert the main effect on the σ values variation. From Table S3, the Ea values were respectively in the range of (22.8–28.1) kJ/mol in the heating process and (23.2–28.2) kJ/mol in the cooling process with a five-cycle of −30 °C–30 °C. The stable Ea values can signify the invariable proton conduction resistance owing to the integrated proton conduction pathways and interconnected network.

Besides that, the long-term σ stability test at subzero temperature was also performed since the hydroxyl radicals preferred to attract sulfonated groups and resulted in the degradation of membranes. The σ values of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes at −30 °C, 0 °C, 30 °C have been measured and the results were shown in Fig. 9 . The σ value at 0 °C in a 315 non-stop test was increased from 1.20 × 10−2 S/cm to 1.85 × 10−2 S/cm within the initial 60 h and then stable around 1.58 × 10−2 S/cm at 0 °C for 315 h. As discussed in the system of SPEEK/PVA/PA, the water molecules liquefaction on membrane surface was the primary reason to cause this phenomenon [28]. The stable σ values with a small fluctuation at −30 °C could support the explanation because the water molecules have frozen. We found that it was stable around 9.34 × 10−3 S/cm until voluntary discontinuation at 315 h. Furthermore, the enhanced σ value at 30 °C was stable at 2.69 × 10−2 S/cm. Furthermore, Zhao CJ mentioned the σ value of the SPAEK/IL@MOF-1 membrane decreasing from 0.184 S/cm to 0.174 S/cm at 80 °C with the test lasting 30 days [63]. Relative to the anhydrous PEM in the elevated temperature [1], the imperceptible PA leakage and the negligible dimension change under low temperature conditions could result in the stable σ values of PA doped membranes.

The mechanical properties of the prepared membranes and PA doped membranes were measured and the tensile stress (E) - strain (ε) curves were respectively shown in Fig. 10 (A) and (B). The maximum E value of the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane was 9.87 MPa, lower than 12.4 MPa of PU membranes and 14.9 MPa of Nafion/CDs membranes. The doped PA molecules inevitably caused a decrease on E values of PA doped membranes. However, the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane was the least affected owing to the multilayered structure. Specifically, (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA, Nafion/CDs/PA and PU/PA membranes exhibited the E values of 4.65 MPa, 4.39 MPa and 5.56 MPa, respectively. The residual proportion was 47.1% of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes, higher than 29.5% of Nafion/CDs/PA and 44.8% of PU/PA membranes. The multilayered structure could help to withstand PA molecules swelling the polymer skeleton of membranes. Notably, the E values of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 and (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes could reach 8.38 MPa and 4.27 MPa even though they were stored at −30 °C for 96 h. Furthermore, the increased density as discussed in the section of 3.7 could compensate for the stiffness decrease of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes.

On the basis of preparing high temperature PEM with the LBL self-assembly technique, this research nanoscopically constructed the LTPEMs through the alternate deposition of PU, Nafion and CDs. Besides the electrostatic attraction between components, the formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds resulted in the successful construction of (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membranes with the compact and multilayered microstructure. Furthermore, the prepared PA doped membranes exhibited the high and stable proton conductivity at subzero temperature, revealing from the ample proton conduction carriers and the components stability. The (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membranes exhibited the proton conductivities of 3.34 × 10−2 S/cm at 30 °C and 3.69 × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C even the membrane underwent a five-cycle heating/cooling process of −30°C–30 °C. Furthermore, the values maintained 9.34 × 10−3 S/cm at −30 °C and 1.58 × 10−2 S/cm at 0 °C even after a 315 h non-stop test. We recommended the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane as the membrane electrolyte for working in the low temperature PEMFC since the results have demonstrated the LBL self-assembly membrane with the potential of application. As a result of the multilayered membranes consisting of carbon dots with the enhanced proton conductivity at subzero temperature, there was a great prospect of the LTPEMs preparation with low-dimensional carbon materials, such as carbon nanotube, graphene oxide, etc.
